summary: The McLain Family Band is my family’s group that recorded music primarily through the 1970s and beyond. Recently, we released 'Volume One' of The McLain Family Band Collection, which includes our first eight albums digitized from original albums. Our father was a huge influence on us, encouraging fearlessness and creativity, and we developed a unique sound blending traditional Appalachian music with other styles. Listening back to these recordings, especially from the 1970s, was emotional and gave me a new appreciation for our legacy and the musical history we created together.

Jeff Myers at Tower Studio in Lexington did a fantastic job digitizing our original McLain Family Band albums. He helped us preserve the quality of these recordings as we prepared to release the collection digitally. My husband worked closely with Jeff to get the files ready, and Jeff’s expertise was crucial in making sure the music came through clearly from the original albums. His work allowed us to revisit and share our early music with new audiences.

Tower Studio in Lexington is where Jeff Myers digitized our original McLain Family Band albums for the new collection release. This studio was instrumental in preserving the sound quality of our recordings from the 1970s. Working with Tower Studio gave us confidence that the legacy of our music would be maintained accurately for future listeners. It was a key part of bringing our old albums into the digital age.

Lexington, Kentucky is where Tower Studio is located, and where Jeff Myers helped us digitize our old McLain Family Band albums. It’s a place connected to the technical side of preserving our family’s musical history. While the band’s roots are in eastern Kentucky, Lexington became important for this project because of the studio and the professionals there who helped us. It’s part of the journey of bringing our 1970s music into the present.

Michael Jonathan, a folk singer and longtime friend, was a big reason we decided to release the full McLain Family Band collection. He encouraged us to see the importance of preserving the entire legacy, not just a 'best of' compilation. Michael has been talking about this project for over ten years and helped us realize that many people beyond our immediate fans would be interested in our music. His support motivated us to produce a comprehensive set that spans from 1970 to now.

Morehead, Kentucky is where I was teaching when I first listened to the digitized McLain Family Band albums on my way there. Hearing those recordings on the road was overwhelming and emotional because it brought back so many memories of our early days as a band and our father’s influence. Morehead represents a moment when I connected deeply with our musical past while balancing my current life as a teacher. It was a powerful experience to revisit those sounds in that place.

Rickey Wasson’s studio is a special place where we recorded because it has booths with windows so we can see each other while performing. That setup helps us maintain the live feel and energy that’s so important to our music. Recording there allows us to sing together and capture the dynamic interaction that’s lost when parts are recorded separately. Rickey’s studio supports the kind of authentic, live sound that reflects how we perform on stage.

Chicago comes up when we talk about modern recording methods where musicians might be in different cities, like someone recording their part in Chicago and uploading it. But for us, that kind of remote recording lacks the live energy we value. We prefer to record together in the same space or at least be able to see each other, which is why studios like Rickey Wasson’s are so important. Chicago represents the contrast between remote digital recording and the live, collaborative approach we cherish.
